A Unified Approach to Routing and Cascading for LLMs

Jasper Dekoninck тАГтАГ Maximilian Baader тАГтАГ Martin Vechev
Abstract

The availability of a wide range of large language models (LLMs) embedded in various agentic systems has significantly increased the potential of model selection strategies to improve the cost-performance tradeoff. Existing strategies involve either routing, where a single model is chosen per query, or cascading, which sequentially runs increasingly larger models until a satisfactory answer is found. However, current approaches face three key limitations: they (1) lack formal proofs of optimality, (2) fail to identify the conditions under which these strategies are most effective to improve the cost-performance tradeoff, and (3) are unable to combine both paradigms for further improvements. To address these issues, we first derive a novel optimal strategy for cascading and prove the optimality of an existing routing strategy. Further, we propose cascade routing, a unified framework that integrates routing and cascading into a theoretically optimal strategy. Through our analysis, we identify good quality estimators as the critical factor for the success of model selection paradigms. Finally, in our experiments, we show that cascade routing consistently outperforms the individual approaches by a large margin and we analyze quality estimators to determine when routing and/or cascading are useful paradigms for model selection.111Code available at https://github.com/eth-sri/cascade-routing

LLMs, Routing, Cascading

1 Introduction

Large language models (LLMs) have found applications in a wide range of tasks, some of which are easily handled by small models, while others require the full capacity of state-of-the-art LLMs. This has led to the development of many fine-tuned models of various sizes that target specific tasks. To maximize performance, it is crucial to select the most suitable model for each query, accounting for both the expected quality of the modelтАЩs output and the modelтАЩs cost. Such model selection strategies can significantly improve performance over any individual model and can reduce inference costs by selecting a smaller model when the query does not require the full capacity of a larger model.

Routing and Cascading

Two primary strategies have been proposed to solve model selection. The first, routing, directs each input query to a specific model from a set of available models (Chen et┬аal., 2022; Liu et┬аal., 2024), as illustrated in Fig.┬а1(a). This approach is particularly effective when different expert LLMs are needed for diverse tasks, enabling the selection of the most suitable expert for each query. The second strategy, cascading, processes an input query through a sequence of increasingly larger models, stopping when a model produces an answer deemed sufficiently good (Chen et┬аal., 2023; Varshney & Baral, 2022), as illustrated in Fig.┬а1(b). Cascading is particularly valuable for handling queries of varying difficulty, as it allows simpler queries to be addressed by smaller models while reserving more complex queries for larger models.

Restrictive Conditions

Despite their utility, both routing and cascading impose significant restrictions on the model selection process. In routing, the initial selection of a model is final, preventing any reconsideration after the initial decision. In cascading, each query must sequentially pass through all models in the chain, with no option to skip a model. Therefore, a less restrictive strategy that combines the strengths of both routing and cascading could offer significant performance improvements.

Lack of Deeper Understanding

Further, the conditions under which current routing and cascading strategies are optimal, are not well understood. For routing, an extensive proof is required just to show that current strategies are close to optimal (Chen et┬аal., 2022), while the theoretical analysis of cascading does not provide optimality guarantees (Chen et┬аal., 2023; Varshney & Baral, 2022). This lack of theoretical understanding hinders the development of more effective model selection strategies. Moreover, prior work fails to provide insights into the limitations of model selection strategies and cannot identify the conditions under which they are useful in practical scenarios. For instance, it is widely believed that one needs models of various sizes to benefit from cascading (Chen et┬аal., 2023; Gupta et┬аal., 2024; Khalili et┬аal., 2022), but we show that this notion is incorrect.

This Work: Cascade Routing

To address these limitations, we first derive optimal routing and cascading strategies by framing them as linear optimization problems aimed at maximizing output quality while remaining within a given cost budget. For routing, this optimal strategy is close to the one obtained by prior work, while for cascading we derive a new strategy that is provably better than existing approaches. Building on this analysis, we propose a new paradigm called cascade routing, which generalizes both routing and cascading. As illustrated in Fig.┬а1(c), cascade routing initially routes a query to any available model but keeps rerouting to different models until a model produces an answer of sufficient quality. We prove the optimality of cascade routing and show that it offers significantly more flexibility in processing a query.

Importance of Quality Estimation

Our theoretical analysis enables a more thorough investigation into the factors that influence the effectiveness of model selection strategies. Specifically, we find that accurate estimates of model performance and response quality are most important. For routing, reliable ex-ante quality estimationтАФthe ability to predict whether a model will perform well on a given queryтАФis essential. For cascading, robust post-hoc quality estimationтАФthe ability to evaluate the quality of a modelтАЩs response after generationтАФis critical. Without it, the effectiveness of cascading strategies is severely limited even when models of various sizes are available.

Results

We evaluate cascade routing on a range of tasks, demonstrating that it significantly outperforms both routing and cascading. Notably, cascade routing consistently outperforms other methods, improving performance by up to 8%percent88\%8 % on the RouterBench benchmark (Hu et┬аal., 2024) and by 14%percent1414\%14 % on the SWE-Bench benchmark (Jimenez et┬аal., 2024). Further, we show that our new cascading strategy outperforms existing cascades in several scenarios by over 10%percent1010\%10 %.

Key Contributions

Our main contributions are:

  • тАв

    We derive optimal strategies for routing and cascading and obtain a new cascading strategy that is provably better than prior approaches (Section┬а2, Section┬а3).

  • тАв

    We introduce cascade routing, a new paradigm that combines the strengths of routing and cascading, and prove its optimality (Section┬а4).

  • тАв

    We conduct a thorough evaluation, demonstrating that cascade routing consistently outperforms the baselines, highlighting the critical role of quality estimation for the effectiveness of model selection (Section┬а5).

2 Routing as Linear Optimization

We derive an optimal routing strategy to select the best model for a given query, providing detailed proofs for all statements in this section in App.┬аA.

Brief Overview

In this section, we begin by defining a routing strategy as a function that maps queries to models. Next, we introduce the notation for quality and cost functions, demonstrating how the optimal routing strategy can be formulated to maximize a linear tradeoff between these two factors. Lastly, we give an illustrative example and discuss the significance of quality estimation in routing and its impact on the effectiveness of the strategy.

Routing

In routing, our goal is to develop a strategy that selects the best language model for a given input query. Formally, let ЁЭТ│ЁЭТ│\mathcal{X}caligraphic_X represent the distribution over all possible queries, and suppose we have kЁЭСШkitalic_k language models m1,тАж,mksubscriptЁЭСЪ1тАжsubscriptЁЭСЪЁЭСШm_{1},\dots,m_{k}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , тАж , italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUBSCRIPT available for routing. Further, let ╬Фksubscript╬ФЁЭСШ\Delta_{k}roman_╬Ф start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUBSCRIPT denote the set of all probability distributions over kЁЭСШkitalic_k variables. A routing strategy can then be defined as follows:

Definition 1 (Routing).

A routing strategy sЁЭСаsitalic_s is a function s:ЁЭТ│тЖТ╬Фk:ЁЭСатЖТЁЭТ│subscript╬ФЁЭСШs\colon\mathcal{X}\rightarrow\Delta_{k}italic_s : caligraphic_X тЖТ roman_╬Ф start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUBSCRIPT that maps a query xтИИЁЭТ│ЁЭСеЁЭТ│x\in\mathcal{X}italic_x тИИ caligraphic_X to a probability distribution over models. siтБв(x)subscriptЁЭСаЁЭСЦЁЭСеs_{i}(x)italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) denotes the probability that misubscriptЁЭСЪЁЭСЦm_{i}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is selected for query xЁЭСеxitalic_x.

A routing strategy selects a model by sampling from the distribution sтБв(x)ЁЭСаЁЭСеs(x)italic_s ( italic_x ) for each query xЁЭСеxitalic_x. In prior work, routing strategies were restricted to be deterministic, i.e., siтБв(x)тИИ{0,1}subscriptЁЭСаЁЭСЦЁЭСе01s_{i}(x)\in\{0,1\}italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) тИИ { 0 , 1 } (Chen et┬аal., 2022; Hu et┬аal., 2024). In contrast, we propose using a more general probabilistic strategy that enables a better solution and an easier theoretical analysis.

Quality and Cost

In routing, we seek to maximize the output quality of the selected model while adhering to a given cost budget BЁЭР╡Bitalic_B. We define the quality function qiтБв(x)subscriptЁЭСЮЁЭСЦЁЭСеq_{i}(x)italic_q start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) as the output quality of model misubscriptЁЭСЪЁЭСЦm_{i}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT on query xЁЭСеxitalic_x, and the cost function ciтБв(x)subscriptЁЭСРЁЭСЦЁЭСеc_{i}(x)italic_c start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) as the cost of running model misubscriptЁЭСЪЁЭСЦm_{i}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT on xЁЭСеxitalic_x. Quality could measure model accuracy, user preference, or any other performance indicator. Cost could measure either monetary costs or latency, depending on the use case.

However, since these functions are unknown in practice, we need estimators q^iтБв(x)subscript^ЁЭСЮЁЭСЦЁЭСе\hat{q}_{i}(x)over^ start_ARG italic_q end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) and c^iтБв(x)subscript^ЁЭСРЁЭСЦЁЭСе\hat{c}_{i}(x)over^ start_ARG italic_c end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) that approximate the output quality and cost of querying model misubscriptЁЭСЪЁЭСЦm_{i}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT on input xЁЭСеxitalic_x. Estimators for qiтБв(x)subscriptЁЭСЮЁЭСЦЁЭСеq_{i}(x)italic_q start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) can be created using small classifiers trained to predict model accuracy, as done in prior work (Hu et┬аal., 2024; Shnitzer et┬аal., 2023). c^iтБв(x)subscript^ЁЭСРЁЭСЦЁЭСе\hat{c}_{i}(x)over^ start_ARG italic_c end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) can be estimated by tokenizing the input query and determining the average output length of the model on a query. Then, we can use API-specific costs per token to estimate the cost of running a model on a query. Alternatively, we can also use average execution time as a proxy for cost.

Optimal Routing

Using these estimators, we can formally define the optimal routing strategy:

Definition 2 (Optimal Routing).

The optimal routing strategy soptsubscriptЁЭСаopts_{\textsc{opt}}italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT opt end_POSTSUBSCRIPT for a given cost budget BЁЭР╡Bitalic_B is the solution to the optimization problem that maximizes the expected output quality of the selected model while adhering to the budget:

maxssubscriptЁЭСа\displaystyle\max_{s}roman_max start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ЁЭФ╝xтИИЁЭТ│тБв(тИСi=1ksiтБв(x)тБвq^iтБв(x))subscriptЁЭФ╝ЁЭСеЁЭТ│superscriptsubscriptЁЭСЦ1ЁЭСШsubscriptЁЭСаЁЭСЦЁЭСеsubscript^ЁЭСЮЁЭСЦЁЭСе\displaystyle\mathbb{E}_{x\in\mathcal{X}}\left(\sum\nolimits_{i=1}^{k}s_{i}(x)% \hat{q}_{i}(x)\right)blackboard_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_x тИИ caligraphic_X end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( тИС start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i = 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) over^ start_ARG italic_q end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) ) (1)
s.t. ЁЭФ╝xтИИЁЭТ│тБв(тИСi=1ksiтБв(x)тБвc^iтБв(x))тй╜B.subscriptЁЭФ╝ЁЭСеЁЭТ│superscriptsubscriptЁЭСЦ1ЁЭСШsubscriptЁЭСаЁЭСЦЁЭСеsubscript^ЁЭСРЁЭСЦЁЭСеЁЭР╡\displaystyle\mathbb{E}_{x\in\mathcal{X}}\left(\sum\nolimits_{i=1}^{k}s_{i}(x)% \hat{c}_{i}(x)\right)\leqslant B.blackboard_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_x тИИ caligraphic_X end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( тИС start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i = 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) over^ start_ARG italic_c end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) ) тй╜ italic_B .

We now explain how to solve this optimization problem. For a given query xЁЭСеxitalic_x, it can be shown (see App.┬аA) that the optimal routing strategy selects the model maximizing the cost-quality tradeoff ╧ДiтБв(x,╬╗)=q^iтБв(x)тИТ╬╗тБвc^iтБв(x)subscriptЁЭЬПЁЭСЦЁЭСеЁЭЬЖsubscript^ЁЭСЮЁЭСЦЁЭСеЁЭЬЖsubscript^ЁЭСРЁЭСЦЁЭСе\tau_{i}(x,\lambda)=\hat{q}_{i}(x)-\lambda\hat{c}_{i}(x)italic_╧Д start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x , italic_╬╗ ) = over^ start_ARG italic_q end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) - italic_╬╗ over^ start_ARG italic_c end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ). Here, ╬╗тИИтДЭ+ЁЭЬЖsuperscriptтДЭ\lambda\in\mathbb{R}^{+}italic_╬╗ тИИ blackboard_R start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT + end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT is a hyperparameter that controls the balance between quality and cost based on the budget BЁЭР╡Bitalic_B.

However, it can occur that several models achieve the same optimal cost-quality tradeoff for a given query. To address this, we define two deterministic strategies smin╬╗тБв(x)superscriptsubscriptЁЭСаminЁЭЬЖЁЭСеs_{\textsc{min}}^{\lambda}(x)italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_╬╗ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x ) and smax╬╗тБв(x)superscriptsubscriptЁЭСаmaxЁЭЬЖЁЭСеs_{\textsc{max}}^{\lambda}(x)italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_╬╗ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x ), which, respectively, select the cheapest and most expensive model that achieves the optimal tradeoff. The optimal routing strategy soptsubscriptЁЭСаopts_{\textsc{opt}}italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT opt end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is then determined by:

Theorem 1 (Optimal Routing Strategy).

For a cost budget BЁЭР╡Bitalic_B, there exists a ╬╗тИИтДЭ+ЁЭЬЖsuperscriptтДЭ\lambda\in\mathbb{R}^{+}italic_╬╗ тИИ blackboard_R start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT + end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT and a ╬│тИИ[0,1]ЁЭЫ╛01\gamma\in[0,1]italic_╬│ тИИ [ 0 , 1 ] such that the optimal routing strategy soptsubscriptЁЭСаopts_{\textsc{opt}}italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT opt end_POSTSUBSCRIPT equals ╬│тБвsmin╬╗+(1тИТ╬│)тБвsmax╬╗ЁЭЫ╛superscriptsubscriptЁЭСаminЁЭЬЖ1ЁЭЫ╛superscriptsubscriptЁЭСаmaxЁЭЬЖ\gamma s_{\textsc{min}}^{\lambda}+(1-\gamma)s_{\textsc{max}}^{\lambda}italic_╬│ italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_╬╗ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT + ( 1 - italic_╬│ ) italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_╬╗ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT. Theorem┬а1, continued. Furthermore, all routing strategies that have an expected cost that is exactly equal to BЁЭР╡Bitalic_B and can be written as a convex combination of smin╬╗тА▓superscriptsubscriptЁЭСаminsuperscriptЁЭЬЖтА▓s_{\textsc{min}}^{\lambda^{\prime}}italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT тА▓ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT and smax╬╗тА▓superscriptsubscriptЁЭСаmaxsuperscriptЁЭЬЖтА▓s_{\textsc{max}}^{\lambda^{\prime}}italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT тА▓ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT for some ╬╗тА▓тИИтДЭ+superscriptЁЭЬЖтА▓superscriptтДЭ\lambda^{\prime}\in\mathbb{R}^{+}italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT тА▓ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT тИИ blackboard_R start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT + end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT achieve the same optimal quality.

In App.┬аA, we show how to obtain the optimal ╬╗ЁЭЬЖ\lambdaitalic_╬╗ and ╬│ЁЭЫ╛\gammaitalic_╬│ for a cost budget BЁЭР╡Bitalic_B using a validation dataset DЁЭР╖Ditalic_D. In Algorithm┬а1, we provide pseudocode for the optimal routing algorithm.

Since ╬│ЁЭЫ╛\gammaitalic_╬│ is often not equal to 00 or 1111, soptsubscriptЁЭСаopts_{\textsc{opt}}italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT opt end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is not necessarily deterministic, i.e., there are queries xЁЭСеxitalic_x such that sopt,iтБв(x)тИЙ{0,1}subscriptЁЭСаoptЁЭСЦЁЭСе01s_{\textsc{opt},i}(x)\notin\{0,1\}italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT opt , italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) тИЙ { 0 , 1 } for some index iЁЭСЦiitalic_i. Therefore, prior work that only considered deterministic routing strategies (Chen et┬аal., 2022; Hu et┬аal., 2024) cannot express the optimal routing strategy and fall back to the near-optimal smin╬╗superscriptsubscriptЁЭСаminЁЭЬЖs_{\textsc{min}}^{\lambda}italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_╬╗ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT.

Example

To illustrate routing, consider a scenario with two models m1subscriptЁЭСЪ1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and m2subscriptЁЭСЪ2m_{2}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. The estimated cost c^тБв(x)=(0.9,1)^ЁЭСРЁЭСе0.91\hat{c}(x)=(0.9,1)over^ start_ARG italic_c end_ARG ( italic_x ) = ( 0.9 , 1 ) is constant across queries and slightly lower for m1subscriptЁЭСЪ1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT than for m2subscriptЁЭСЪ2m_{2}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. The quality is estimated based on the category of the query, which is either math, code, or generic. For instance, let q^тБв(xmath)=(0.8,0.5)^ЁЭСЮsubscriptЁЭСеmath0.80.5\hat{q}(x_{\textrm{math}})=(0.8,0.5)over^ start_ARG italic_q end_ARG ( italic_x start_POSTSUBSCRIPT math end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) = ( 0.8 , 0.5 ), q^тБв(xcode)=(0.5,0.8)^ЁЭСЮsubscriptЁЭСеcode0.50.8\hat{q}(x_{\textrm{code}})=(0.5,0.8)over^ start_ARG italic_q end_ARG ( italic_x start_POSTSUBSCRIPT code end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) = ( 0.5 , 0.8 ), and q^тБв(xgeneric)=(0.8,0.9)^ЁЭСЮsubscriptЁЭСеgeneric0.80.9\hat{q}(x_{\textrm{generic}})=(0.8,0.9)over^ start_ARG italic_q end_ARG ( italic_x start_POSTSUBSCRIPT generic end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) = ( 0.8 , 0.9 ). For ╬╗=1ЁЭЬЖ1\lambda=1italic_╬╗ = 1 and ╬│=0.7ЁЭЫ╛0.7\gamma=0.7italic_╬│ = 0.7, the cost-quality tradeoff is highest for m1subscriptЁЭСЪ1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, resp. m2subscriptЁЭСЪ2m_{2}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, on math, resp. code, queries, and is equal for both models on generic queries. Thus, the router will select m1subscriptЁЭСЪ1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, resp. m2subscriptЁЭСЪ2m_{2}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, for math, resp. code, queries, and select m1subscriptЁЭСЪ1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT with a probability of 0.70.70.70.7 and m2subscriptЁЭСЪ2m_{2}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT with a probability of 0.30.30.30.3 for generic queries.

Ex-Ante Quality Estimation

We explicitly distinguish between the modelтАЩs true quality qiтБв(x)subscriptЁЭСЮЁЭСЦЁЭСеq_{i}(x)italic_q start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) and the ex-ante quality estimate q^iтБв(x)subscript^ЁЭСЮЁЭСЦЁЭСе\hat{q}_{i}(x)over^ start_ARG italic_q end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ). An optimal routing strategy will select a good model only if qiтБв(x)тЙИq^iтБв(x)subscriptЁЭСЮЁЭСЦЁЭСеsubscript^ЁЭСЮЁЭСЦЁЭСеq_{i}(x)\approx\hat{q}_{i}(x)italic_q start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) тЙИ over^ start_ARG italic_q end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ), otherwise the objective in Eq.┬а1 is not appropriate. Thus, even when routing is suited for the application, the strategy will fail if the quality estimates are inaccurate. This makes quality estimation a critical component of routing strategies. While cost estimation also faces similar challenges, we found that it is less critical and can be approximated more easily.

3 Cascading as Sequential Routing

We extend our analysis of the optimal routing strategy to a cascade, providing proofs for all statements in App.┬аB.

Brief Overview

In this section, we reinterpret cascading as a sequence of routing problems. Furthermore, we show how our approach improves upon the cascading strategies used in prior work. Finally, we examine the impact of post-hoc quality estimates on the effectiveness of cascading strategies.

Cascading

In cascading, an input query is processed sequentially through a chain of models, typically arranged in order of increasing size or cost. The cascade stops once a modelтАЩs output meets a certain condition, and that output is returned. We will reinterpret cascading as a sequence of routing problems. To do so, we first define the models over which we need to route, which we refer to as supermodels.

Definition 3 (Supermodel).

A supermodel MЁЭСАMitalic_M is a sequence of models (mi1,тАж,mij)subscriptЁЭСЪsubscriptЁЭСЦ1тАжsubscriptЁЭСЪsubscriptЁЭСЦЁЭСЧ(m_{i_{1}},\dots,m_{i_{j}})( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , тАж , italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) such that running a query through MЁЭСАMitalic_M is equivalent to running it through each of the models in the sequence. тД│тД│\mathcal{M}caligraphic_M denotes the set of all supermodels and by Mi:jsubscriptЁЭСА:ЁЭСЦЁЭСЧM_{i:j}italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i : italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT we denote the supermodel (mi,тАж,mj)subscriptЁЭСЪЁЭСЦтАжsubscriptЁЭСЪЁЭСЧ(m_{i},\dots,m_{j})( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , тАж , italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ).

In cascading, we only need to consider the supermodels M1:1,тАж,M1:ksubscriptЁЭСА:11тАжsubscriptЁЭСА:1ЁЭСШM_{1:1},\dots,M_{1:k}italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 : 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , тАж , italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 : italic_k end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. The full expressivity of Definition┬а3 will only be necessary for cascade routing in Section┬а4.

Cascading as Sequential Routing

Running a cascade on a sample xЁЭСеxitalic_x occurs in a sequence of steps, where at each step, the cascade determines whether to run the next model in the sequence or terminate. By step jЁЭСЧjitalic_j, we have obtained outputs from the first jтИТ1ЁЭСЧ1j-1italic_j - 1 models. To decide whether to continue and run mjsubscriptЁЭСЪЁЭСЧm_{j}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, we need to determine, in expectation, how well the supermodels M1:jтИТ1,тАжтБвM1:ksubscriptЁЭСА:1ЁЭСЧ1тАжsubscriptЁЭСА:1ЁЭСШM_{1:j-1},\dots M_{1:k}italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 : italic_j - 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , тАж italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 : italic_k end_POSTSUBSCRIPT will perform on the sample xЁЭСеxitalic_x. Once again, this performance is measured as having the highest expected output quality within a certain cost budget. If M1:jтИТ1subscriptЁЭСА:1ЁЭСЧ1M_{1:j-1}italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 : italic_j - 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT offers the best performance, we terminate the cascade and return its output, i.e., the output of mjтИТ1subscriptЁЭСЪЁЭСЧ1m_{j-1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j - 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. Otherwise, if any of M1:j,тАж,M1:ksubscriptЁЭСА:1ЁЭСЧтАжsubscriptЁЭСА:1ЁЭСШM_{1:j},\dots,M_{1:k}italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 : italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , тАж , italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 : italic_k end_POSTSUBSCRIPT has better performance, we continue the cascade and run mjsubscriptЁЭСЪЁЭСЧm_{j}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. Therefore, at step jЁЭСЧjitalic_j, the cascade is equivalent to a routing strategy that selects the best supermodel from M1:jтИТ1,тАж,M1:ksubscriptЁЭСА:1ЁЭСЧ1тАжsubscriptЁЭСА:1ЁЭСШM_{1:j-1},\dots,M_{1:k}italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 : italic_j - 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , тАж , italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 : italic_k end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. Thus, a cascade can be formally defined as follows:

Definition 4 (Cascading Strategy).

A cascading strategy sЁЭСаsitalic_s is a sequence of routing strategies (s(1),тАж,s(k))superscriptЁЭСа1тАжsuperscriptЁЭСаЁЭСШ(s^{(1)},\dots,s^{(k)})( italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 1 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , тАж , italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_k ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) such that s(j)superscriptЁЭСаЁЭСЧs^{(j)}italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_j ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT routes between the supermodels M1:jтИТ1,тАж,M1:ksubscriptЁЭСА:1ЁЭСЧ1тАжsubscriptЁЭСА:1ЁЭСШM_{1:j-1},\dots,M_{1:k}italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 : italic_j - 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , тАж , italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 : italic_k end_POSTSUBSCRIPT.

Notably, while the action associated with supermodels M1:j,тАж,M1:ksubscriptЁЭСА:1ЁЭСЧтАжsubscriptЁЭСА:1ЁЭСШM_{1:j},\dots,M_{1:k}italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 : italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , тАж , italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 : italic_k end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is the same, namely continuing the cascade, it is important to consider all these supermodels. Indeed, a model mjsubscriptЁЭСЪЁЭСЧm_{j}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT might perform poorly while mj+1subscriptЁЭСЪЁЭСЧ1m_{j+1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j + 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT performs exceptionally well on a given query. In such cases, the quality-cost tradeoff of M1:jsubscriptЁЭСА:1ЁЭСЧM_{1:j}italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 : italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT will be worse than the tradeoff of M1:jтИТ1subscriptЁЭСА:1ЁЭСЧ1M_{1:j-1}italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 : italic_j - 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, but M1:j+1subscriptЁЭСА:1ЁЭСЧ1M_{1:j+1}italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 : italic_j + 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT could still provide a better outcome. Therefore, it is crucial to consider all supermodels M1:j,тАж,M1:ksubscriptЁЭСА:1ЁЭСЧтАжsubscriptЁЭСА:1ЁЭСШM_{1:j},\dots,M_{1:k}italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 : italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , тАж , italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 : italic_k end_POSTSUBSCRIPT at step jЁЭСЧjitalic_j rather than making decisions solely based on immediate performance.

Quality and Cost

To apply Theorem┬а1 to find the optimal cascading strategy, we first need to derive the quality and cost estimates of the supermodels. Both of these can depend on the answers of previously computed models. Therefore, let q^(j)тБв(x)superscript^ЁЭСЮЁЭСЧЁЭСе\hat{q}^{(j)}(x)over^ start_ARG italic_q end_ARG start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_j ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x ) and c^(j)тБв(x)superscript^ЁЭСРЁЭСЧЁЭСе\hat{c}^{(j)}(x)over^ start_ARG italic_c end_ARG start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_j ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x ) represent the updated estimates in step jЁЭСЧjitalic_j after computing the first jтИТ1ЁЭСЧ1j-1italic_j - 1 models.

We derive the quality and cost estimates associated with supermodel M1:isubscriptЁЭСА:1ЁЭСЦM_{1:i}italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 : italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, denoted as q^1:i(j)тБв(x)subscriptsuperscript^ЁЭСЮЁЭСЧ:1ЁЭСЦЁЭСе\hat{q}^{(j)}_{1:i}(x)over^ start_ARG italic_q end_ARG start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_j ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 : italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) and c^1:i(j)тБв(x)subscriptsuperscript^ЁЭСРЁЭСЧ:1ЁЭСЦЁЭСе\hat{c}^{(j)}_{1:i}(x)over^ start_ARG italic_c end_ARG start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_j ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 : italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ), based on the quality and cost estimates of the individual models. Trivially, the cost of the supermodel is equal to the sum of the individual model costs. The quality of a supermodel, however, is governed by the best model within it. Thus, it equals ЁЭФ╝q^iтБв[maxтБб(q^1тБв(x),тАж,q^iтБв(x))]subscriptЁЭФ╝subscript^ЁЭСЮЁЭСЦdelimited-[]subscript^ЁЭСЮ1ЁЭСетАжsubscript^ЁЭСЮЁЭСЦЁЭСе\mathbb{E}_{\hat{q}_{i}}[\max(\hat{q}_{1}(x),\dots,\hat{q}_{i}(x))]blackboard_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT over^ start_ARG italic_q end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUBSCRIPT [ roman_max ( over^ start_ARG italic_q end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) , тАж , over^ start_ARG italic_q end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) ) ], where the expected value reflects the uncertainty in each quality estimate. Specifically, each quality estimate q^iтБв(x)subscript^ЁЭСЮЁЭСЦЁЭСе\hat{q}_{i}(x)over^ start_ARG italic_q end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) is modeled as a random variable estimating the true quality qiтБв(x)subscriptЁЭСЮЁЭСЦЁЭСеq_{i}(x)italic_q start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ). This is crucial since ignoring uncertainty would falsely assume that the quality of a supermodel is always equal to the best model within it, even though the best model may return a poor answer, while another returns a good one. To estimate the uncertainties associated with the estimates, we compute the variance of q^i(j)тБв(x)тИТq^i(k)тБв(x)subscriptsuperscript^ЁЭСЮЁЭСЧЁЭСЦЁЭСеsubscriptsuperscript^ЁЭСЮЁЭСШЁЭСЦЁЭСе\hat{q}^{(j)}_{i}(x)-\hat{q}^{(k)}_{i}(x)over^ start_ARG italic_q end_ARG start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_j ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) - over^ start_ARG italic_q end_ARG start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_k ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) over a validation dataset.

Optimal Cascading

We now leverage the optimal routing strategy from Theorem┬а1 to determine the optimal cascading strategy. As before, optimality is defined in terms of maximizing the expected output quality while adhering to a given cost budget. However, the budget is now only enforced over the entire cascade, and not over individual steps. This leads to a slightly different formulation:

Theorem 2 (Optimal Cascading Strategy).

For a given cost budget BЁЭР╡Bitalic_B, there exist ╬╗1,тАж,╬╗kтИИтДЭ+subscriptЁЭЬЖ1тАжsubscriptЁЭЬЖЁЭСШsuperscriptтДЭ\lambda_{1},\dots,\lambda_{k}\in\mathbb{R}^{+}italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , тАж , italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUBSCRIPT тИИ blackboard_R start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT + end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT and a ╬│тИИ[0,1]ЁЭЫ╛01\gamma\in[0,1]italic_╬│ тИИ [ 0 , 1 ] such that the optimal cascading strategy sopt=(sopt(1),тАж,sopt(k))subscriptЁЭСаoptsubscriptsuperscriptЁЭСа1optтАжsubscriptsuperscriptЁЭСаЁЭСШopts_{\textsc{opt}}=(s^{(1)}_{\textsc{opt}},\dots,s^{(k)}_{\textsc{opt}})italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT opt end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = ( italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 1 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT opt end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , тАж , italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_k ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT opt end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) is given by sopt(j)=╬│тБвsmin(j),╬╗j+(1тИТ╬│)тБвsmax(j),╬╗jsubscriptsuperscriptЁЭСаЁЭСЧoptЁЭЫ╛subscriptsuperscriptЁЭСаЁЭСЧsubscriptЁЭЬЖЁЭСЧmin1ЁЭЫ╛subscriptsuperscriptЁЭСаЁЭСЧsubscriptЁЭЬЖЁЭСЧmaxs^{(j)}_{\textsc{opt}}=\gamma s^{(j),\lambda_{j}}_{\textsc{min}}+(1-\gamma)s^{% (j),\lambda_{j}}_{\textsc{max}}italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_j ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT opt end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = italic_╬│ italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_j ) , italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT + ( 1 - italic_╬│ ) italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_j ) , italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT where smin(j),╬╗jsubscriptsuperscriptЁЭСаЁЭСЧsubscriptЁЭЬЖЁЭСЧmins^{(j),\lambda_{j}}_{\textsc{min}}italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_j ) , italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and smax(j),╬╗jsubscriptsuperscriptЁЭСаЁЭСЧsubscriptЁЭЬЖЁЭСЧmaxs^{(j),\lambda_{j}}_{\textsc{max}}italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_j ) , italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT are defined as in Theorem┬а1.

In App.┬аB, we explain how to obtain the hyperparameters ╬╗1,тАж,╬╗ksubscriptЁЭЬЖ1тАжsubscriptЁЭЬЖЁЭСШ\lambda_{1},\dots,\lambda_{k}italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , тАж , italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and ╬│ЁЭЫ╛\gammaitalic_╬│ for a given cost budget BЁЭР╡Bitalic_B using a validation dataset DЁЭР╖Ditalic_D. In Algorithm┬а2, we provide pseudocode for the optimal cascading algorithm, illustrating the steps involved in selecting the best model for a given query.

Example

To illustrate the optimal cascading strategy, consider once again a scenario with two models m1,m2subscriptЁЭСЪ1subscriptЁЭСЪ2m_{1},m_{2}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT with costs c^(1)тБв(x)=(0.5,1)superscript^ЁЭСР1ЁЭСе0.51\hat{c}^{(1)}(x)=(0.5,1)over^ start_ARG italic_c end_ARG start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 1 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x ) = ( 0.5 , 1 ) and q^(1)тБв(x)=(0.5,0.8)superscript^ЁЭСЮ1ЁЭСе0.50.8\hat{q}^{(1)}(x)=(0.5,0.8)over^ start_ARG italic_q end_ARG start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 1 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x ) = ( 0.5 , 0.8 ) (ignoring uncertainty). In a cascade, we will always run m1subscriptЁЭСЪ1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT for a query xЁЭСеxitalic_x. Thus, we obtain the first modelтАЩs output. Based on this output, we can adjust the quality and cost estimates. Suppose, for instance, that the model output is very long and its confidence in its own answer is only 30%percent3030\%30 %. Then we update, for instance, q^(2)тБв(x)=(0.3,0.6)superscript^ЁЭСЮ2ЁЭСе0.30.6\hat{q}^{(2)}(x)=(0.3,0.6)over^ start_ARG italic_q end_ARG start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x ) = ( 0.3 , 0.6 ) and c^(2)тБв(x)=(1,2)superscript^ЁЭСР2ЁЭСе12\hat{c}^{(2)}(x)=(1,2)over^ start_ARG italic_c end_ARG start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x ) = ( 1 , 2 ). For ╬╗2=1subscriptЁЭЬЖ21\lambda_{2}=1italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 1, we would now stop the cascade and return the output of m1subscriptЁЭСЪ1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT since the cost-quality tradeoff is highest for the supermodel {m1}subscriptЁЭСЪ1\{m_{1}\}{ italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT }. If, instead, ╬╗2=0.1subscriptЁЭЬЖ20.1\lambda_{2}=0.1italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 0.1, we would run m2subscriptЁЭСЪ2m_{2}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT since the cost-quality tradeoff is highest for the supermodel {m1,m2}subscriptЁЭСЪ1subscriptЁЭСЪ2\{m_{1},m_{2}\}{ italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT }. In this case, the cascade would return the output of m2subscriptЁЭСЪ2m_{2}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT.

Prior Work

Prior work on cascading has often relied on strong assumptions to simplify the strategy, using a treshold-strategy as an approximation of the optimal cascade. Specifically, in step jЁЭСЧjitalic_j, the cascade continues if q^jтИТ1(j)тБв(x)<╧Дjsubscriptsuperscript^ЁЭСЮЁЭСЧЁЭСЧ1ЁЭСеsubscriptЁЭЬПЁЭСЧ\hat{q}^{(j)}_{j-1}(x)<\tau_{j}over^ start_ARG italic_q end_ARG start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_j ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j - 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) < italic_╧Д start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT for some threshold ╧ДjтИИтДЭsubscriptЁЭЬПЁЭСЧтДЭ\tau_{j}\in\mathbb{R}italic_╧Д start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT тИИ blackboard_R. To the best of our knowledge, all existing works can be seen as a specific instantiation of this thresholding scheme with cost and quality estimators that depend on the specific application used (Chen et┬аal., 2023; Damani et┬аal., 2024; Gupta et┬аal., 2024; Jitkrittum et┬аal., 2023b; Nie et┬аal., 2024). Below, we outline the conditions under which this simplified approach is optimal.

Corollary 1 (Optimal Threshold Strategy).

Under minor technical assumptions, the thresholding strategy is equivalent to our cascading strategy if and only if the following conditions hold: c^i(j)тБв(x)subscriptsuperscript^ЁЭСРЁЭСЧЁЭСЦЁЭСе\hat{c}^{(j)}_{i}(x)over^ start_ARG italic_c end_ARG start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_j ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) is independent of xЁЭСеxitalic_x for all i,jтИИ{1,тАж,k}ЁЭСЦЁЭСЧ1тАжЁЭСШi,j\in\{1,\dots,k\}italic_i , italic_j тИИ { 1 , тАж , italic_k }, q^i(j)тБв(x)subscriptsuperscript^ЁЭСЮЁЭСЧЁЭСЦЁЭСе\hat{q}^{(j)}_{i}(x)over^ start_ARG italic_q end_ARG start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_j ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) is independent of xЁЭСеxitalic_x for all iтй╛jЁЭСЦЁЭСЧi\geqslant jitalic_i тй╛ italic_j, and q^1:i(j)тБв(x)subscriptsuperscript^ЁЭСЮЁЭСЧ:1ЁЭСЦЁЭСе\hat{q}^{(j)}_{1:i}(x)over^ start_ARG italic_q end_ARG start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_j ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 : italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) is equal to q^i(j)тБв(x)subscriptsuperscript^ЁЭСЮЁЭСЧЁЭСЦЁЭСе\hat{q}^{(j)}_{i}(x)over^ start_ARG italic_q end_ARG start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_j ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ).

Post-Hoc Quality Estimation

Once again, this theoretical framework highlights the importance of quality estimation, with a shift in focus from ex-ante quality estimation to post-hoc quality estimation, which now plays a critical role. Cascading approaches are only advantageous when the post-hoc quality estimate provides significantly better information than the ex-ante estimate. If this improvement is minimal, it would be more effective to directly route queries to the most suitable model, bypassing the cascading process. While the post-hoc estimate is essential for refining decisions, the ex-ante quality estimate remains valuable in determining whether future models can potentially deliver better performance. Only in the threshold cascade strategy, where the ex-ante estimate is fixed, does it become irrelevant. By contrast, our approach improves upon the threshold cascade by incorporating both ex-ante and post-hoc quality estimates, thereby enabling more informed decision-making.

4 Cascade Routing as Cascade Generalization

Both routing and cascading are powerful techniques that enable the efficient use of multiple models. However, their use is often orthogonal: while routing is useful when ex-ante quality estimates are accurate, cascading is more beneficial when post-hoc estimates are accurate. We therefore present cascade routing, which is a generalization of both techniques. Proofs for all theorems and lemmas in this section are included in App.┬аC.

Brief Overview

In this section, we first define cascade routing and explain how it generalizes both routing and cascading. We then derive the optimal cascade routing strategy and solve several of the additional challenges that arise when applying cascade routing in practice. Finally, we provide an illustrative example.

Cascade Routing

Cascade routing closely resembles cascading, but with one crucial difference: the routing strategy at step jЁЭСЧjitalic_j routes between all possible supermodels, not just the supermodels M1:jтИТ1,тАж,M1:ksubscriptЁЭСА:1ЁЭСЧ1тАжsubscriptЁЭСА:1ЁЭСШM_{1:j-1},\dots,M_{1:k}italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 : italic_j - 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , тАж , italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 : italic_k end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. Therefore, both Definition┬а4 and Theorem┬а2 can be extended to this setting.

Definition 5 (Cascade Routing).

A cascade routing strategy sЁЭСаsitalic_s is a sequence of routing strategies (s(1),тАж,s(k))superscriptЁЭСа1тАжsuperscriptЁЭСаЁЭСШ(s^{(1)},\dots,s^{(k)})( italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 1 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , тАж , italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_k ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) such that, for a given sample xтИИЁЭТ│ЁЭСеЁЭТ│x\in\mathcal{X}italic_x тИИ caligraphic_X, s(j)superscriptЁЭСаЁЭСЧs^{(j)}italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_j ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT routes between all supermodels in тД│тД│\mathcal{M}caligraphic_M that start with the jтИТ1ЁЭСЧ1j-1italic_j - 1 models that have already been computed for this query.

Theorem 3 (Optimal Cascade Routing).

For a given cost budget BЁЭР╡Bitalic_B, there exist ╬╗1,тАж,╬╗kтИИтДЭ+subscriptЁЭЬЖ1тАжsubscriptЁЭЬЖЁЭСШsuperscriptтДЭ\lambda_{1},\dots,\lambda_{k}\in\mathbb{R}^{+}italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , тАж , italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUBSCRIPT тИИ blackboard_R start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT + end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT and a ╬│тИИтДЭ+ЁЭЫ╛superscriptтДЭ\gamma\in\mathbb{R}^{+}italic_╬│ тИИ blackboard_R start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT + end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT such that the optimal cascade routing strategy sopt=(sopt(1),тАж,sopt(k))subscriptЁЭСаoptsubscriptsuperscriptЁЭСа1optтАжsubscriptsuperscriptЁЭСаЁЭСШopts_{\textsc{opt}}=(s^{(1)}_{\textsc{opt}},\dots,s^{(k)}_{\textsc{opt}})italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT opt end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = ( italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 1 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT opt end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , тАж , italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_k ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT opt end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) is given by sopt(j)=╬│тБвsmin(j),╬╗j+(1тИТ╬│)тБвsmax(j),╬╗jsubscriptsuperscriptЁЭСаЁЭСЧoptЁЭЫ╛subscriptsuperscriptЁЭСаЁЭСЧsubscriptЁЭЬЖЁЭСЧmin1ЁЭЫ╛subscriptsuperscriptЁЭСаЁЭСЧsubscriptЁЭЬЖЁЭСЧmaxs^{(j)}_{\textsc{opt}}=\gamma s^{(j),\lambda_{j}}_{\textsc{min}}+(1-\gamma)s^{% (j),\lambda_{j}}_{\textsc{max}}italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_j ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT opt end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = italic_╬│ italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_j ) , italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT + ( 1 - italic_╬│ ) italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_j ) , italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT where smin(j),╬╗jsubscriptsuperscriptЁЭСаЁЭСЧsubscriptЁЭЬЖЁЭСЧmins^{(j),\lambda_{j}}_{\textsc{min}}italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_j ) , italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and smax(j),╬╗jsubscriptsuperscriptЁЭСаЁЭСЧsubscriptЁЭЬЖЁЭСЧmaxs^{(j),\lambda_{j}}_{\textsc{max}}italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_j ) , italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT are defined as in Theorem┬а1.

In Algorithm┬а3, we provide pseudocode for the cascade routing algorithm. While cascade routing is a seemingly simple extension of cascading, it also introduces additional challenges which we address now.

Model Order

In cascading, the model order is predetermined, and the routing strategy only decides whether to proceed with the next model in the sequence. In contrast, cascade routing must dynamically determine the order in which models are computed. Despite this, both the estimated quality q^M(j)тБв(x)subscriptsuperscript^ЁЭСЮЁЭСЧЁЭСАЁЭСе\hat{q}^{(j)}_{M}(x)over^ start_ARG italic_q end_ARG start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_j ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_M end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) and cost c^M(j)тБв(x)subscriptsuperscript^ЁЭСРЁЭСЧЁЭСАЁЭСе\hat{c}^{(j)}_{M}(x)over^ start_ARG italic_c end_ARG start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_j ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_M end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) of a supermodel MЁЭСАMitalic_M are order-independent. Therefore, supermodels that contain the same models in a different order will have the same cost and quality. To mitigate this, we sort the models within the selected supermodel by cost and compute the cheapest one first (illustrated in Lines 12-13 of Algorithm┬а3). This approach aligns with cascading, where more expensive models are only used if cheaper models do not suffice.

Number of Supermodels

In cascading, the quality and cost must be computed for a maximum of kЁЭСШkitalic_k supermodels at each step. However, in cascade routing, the number of supermodels grows exponentially, leading to the need to evaluate up to 2ksuperscript2ЁЭСШ2^{k}2 start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT supermodels. This increase can become prohibitively costly, particularly since the model selection process must remain computationally negligible with respect to model computation. To mitigate this, we leverage so-called negative marginal gains. It can be shown (see App.┬аC) that if a model mЁЭСЪmitalic_m in a supermodel MЁЭСАMitalic_M negatively impacts the quality-cost tradeoff, all supermodels containing all models in MЁЭСАMitalic_M can be pruned from the search space. For example, if m1subscriptЁЭСЪ1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT negatively affects the quality-cost tradeoff of the supermodel {m1,m2}subscriptЁЭСЪ1subscriptЁЭСЪ2\{m_{1},m_{2}\}{ italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT }, we can prune all supermodels that contain both m1subscriptЁЭСЪ1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and m2subscriptЁЭСЪ2m_{2}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. Since this negative contribution is quite common, this allows us to prune the search space significantly. More formally, this pruning operation relies on the following lemma:

Lemma 1 (Negative Marginal Gain).

Let MтИИтД│ЁЭСАтД│M\in\mathcal{M}italic_M тИИ caligraphic_M and mЁЭСЪmitalic_m be any model in MЁЭСАMitalic_M. Let the marginal gain of mЁЭСЪmitalic_m w.r.t. MЁЭСАMitalic_M be defined as ╧ДMтБв(x,╬╗)тИТ╧ДMтИЦ{m}тБв(x,╬╗)subscriptЁЭЬПЁЭСАЁЭСеЁЭЬЖsubscriptЁЭЬПЁЭСАЁЭСЪЁЭСеЁЭЬЖ\tau_{M}(x,\lambda)-\tau_{M\setminus\{m\}}(x,\lambda)italic_╧Д start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_M end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x , italic_╬╗ ) - italic_╧Д start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_M тИЦ { italic_m } end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x , italic_╬╗ ). Then, if the marginal gain of mЁЭСЪmitalic_m w.r.t. MЁЭСАMitalic_M is strictly negative for a given query, the optimal cascade routing strategy will never run a supermodel MтА▓тИИтД│superscriptЁЭСАтА▓тД│M^{\prime}\in\mathcal{M}italic_M start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT тА▓ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT тИИ caligraphic_M that contains all models in MЁЭСАMitalic_M.

Example

To illustrate the optimal cascade routing strategy, consider a scenario with two models m1subscriptЁЭСЪ1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and m2subscriptЁЭСЪ2m_{2}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT with costs c^(1)тБв(x)=(0.5,1)superscript^ЁЭСР1ЁЭСе0.51\hat{c}^{(1)}(x)=(0.5,1)over^ start_ARG italic_c end_ARG start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 1 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x ) = ( 0.5 , 1 ) and q^(1)тБв(x)=(0.5,0.8)superscript^ЁЭСЮ1ЁЭСе0.50.8\hat{q}^{(1)}(x)=(0.5,0.8)over^ start_ARG italic_q end_ARG start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 1 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x ) = ( 0.5 , 0.8 ). In contrast to cascading, we do not necessarily need to run m1subscriptЁЭСЪ1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT first. In this scenario, if ╬╗1=0.1subscriptЁЭЬЖ10.1\lambda_{1}=0.1italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 0.1, we would immediately run m2subscriptЁЭСЪ2m_{2}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. While we would most likely stop after m2subscriptЁЭСЪ2m_{2}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, it is possible that its output is so bad that we update the quality estimator to q^(2)тБв(x)=(0.5,0.1)superscript^ЁЭСЮ2ЁЭСе0.50.1\hat{q}^{(2)}(x)=(0.5,0.1)over^ start_ARG italic_q end_ARG start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x ) = ( 0.5 , 0.1 ). In this case, for ╬╗2=0.1subscriptЁЭЬЖ20.1\lambda_{2}=0.1italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 0.1, we would run m1subscriptЁЭСЪ1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT next and return its output. If ╬╗1=1subscriptЁЭЬЖ11\lambda_{1}=1italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 1, we come into the more classical cascading scenario explained before where m1subscriptЁЭСЪ1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is run first.

5 Experimental Evaluation

We now evaluate the performance of cascade routing and demonstrate that it significantly outperforms all other strategies. Additionally, we show that our new cascading approach outperforms the threshold-based cascading method. For this purpose, we first conduct experiments on RouterBench (Hu et┬аal., 2024), a benchmark specifically designed to evaluate routing and cascading (Section┬а5.1). Next, we test cascade routing on several additional benchmarks to evaluate its performance in more realistic scenarios (Section┬а5.2). In the appendix, we perform an ablation study to examine the impact of various design choices in cascade routing on performance and runtime (App.┬аF). Finally, in App.┬аH, we show detailed results as well as cost-quality tradeoff curves for several benchmarks.

5.1 RouterBench

RouterBench (Hu et┬аal., 2024) is a benchmark developed to evaluate the efficacy of different model selection strategies. It includes questions from seven diverse benchmarks, such as MMLU (Hendrycks et┬аal., 2021), GSM8k (Cobbe et┬аal., 2021), and MBPP (Austin et┬аal., 2021), alongside answers from eleven different models ranging from GPT-4 (OpenAI, 2023) to Mistral-7B (Jiang et┬аal., 2023).

Quality and Cost Estimates

Similar to (Hu et┬аal., 2024), we estimate quality and cost by adding zero-centered Gaussian noise to their true values. Both cost and quality estimates are modeled as linear functions fitted on these noisy signals. Thus, the quality estimate can be expressed as q^W,bтБв(x)=WтБв(qтБв(x)+╧╡)+bsubscript^ЁЭСЮЁЭСКЁЭСПЁЭСеЁЭСКЁЭСЮЁЭСеitalic-╧╡ЁЭСП\hat{q}_{W,b}(x)=W(q(x)+\epsilon)+bover^ start_ARG italic_q end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_W , italic_b end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) = italic_W ( italic_q ( italic_x ) + italic_╧╡ ) + italic_b where ╧╡тИ╝ЁЭТйтБв(0,╧Г2)similar-toitalic-╧╡ЁЭТй0superscriptЁЭЬО2\epsilon\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^{2})italic_╧╡ тИ╝ caligraphic_N ( 0 , italic_╧Г start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ). A similar expression holds for the cost estimate. We define the variance of the noisy signal as ╧Гante2superscriptsubscriptЁЭЬОante2\sigma_{\text{ante}}^{2}italic_╧Г start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ante end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT before model computation (ex-ante estimates) and ╧Гpost2superscriptsubscriptЁЭЬОpost2\sigma_{\text{post}}^{2}italic_╧Г start_POSTSUBSCRIPT post end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT after (post-hoc estimates).

To explore different uncertainty levels, we vary the variances to simulate low-, medium-, and high-noise scenarios, with exact values for the variances given in Section┬аD.1.

Models

We evaluate cascade routing on RouterBench using three, five, and eleven models available for model selection, ensuring a comprehensive evaluation across a range of scenarios. The exact models are provided in Section┬аD.1.

Strategies

We compare cascade routing against several baseline strategies, including the routing strategy described in Section┬а2, the threshold-based cascading approach from prior work (Corollary┬а1), and the optimal cascading strategy (Theorem┬а2). Additionally, as in (Hu et┬аal., 2024), we include a baseline that linearly interpolates cost and quality on the Pareto frontier of the models.

Evaluation Metric

For each method, we evaluate performance using cost budgets ranging from the cheapest to the most expensive model. This produces a quality-cost curve for each strategy. Following (Hu et┬аal., 2024), we use the Area Under the Curve (AUC) as the performance metric.

Results

Table┬а1 presents the results for the zero-shot setting, with the five-shot results detailed in App.┬аH. Cascade routing consistently outperforms all baseline strategies with performance gains between 1%percent11\%1 % to 4%percent44\%4 %, which measured relatively to the naive linear interpolation baseline means that cascade routing improves by 13%percent1313\%13 % to 80%percent8080\%80 % over the baselines. This performance gap widens as more models are available and narrows under higher noise levels, indicating that cascade routing is most effective with large model sets and accurate cost and quality estimates. Furthermore, our new cascading strategy outperforms the threshold-based cascade by up to 2%percent22\%2 %, reinforcing the practical relevance of our theoretical results.

Quality Estimation

To better understand the impact of quality estimation on model selection strategies, we additionally conduct experiments with five models under a broader range of varying noise levels. Fig.┬а2 illustrates the difference in AUC performance between cascade routing and baseline strategies for all possible noise levels. The results demonstrate that cascade routing consistently outperforms the baselines, achieving up to an 8%percent88\%8 % improvement for cascading and up to a 12%percent1212\%12 % improvement for routing. Notably, the performance gap highlights key differences between the cascading and routing strategies. For routing, the value of ╧ГantesubscriptЁЭЬОante\sigma_{\text{ante}}italic_╧Г start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ante end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is criticalтАФhigh ╧ГantesubscriptЁЭЬОante\sigma_{\text{ante}}italic_╧Г start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ante end_POSTSUBSCRIPT significantly reduces performance compared to cascade routing. Conversely, for cascading, ╧ГpostsubscriptЁЭЬОpost\sigma_{\text{post}}italic_╧Г start_POSTSUBSCRIPT post end_POSTSUBSCRIPT plays a more influential role, with higher values causing substantial performance degradation. These findings underscore the importance of accurate quality estimation for both strategies. Cascade routing proves to be a more robust solution by unifying the strengths of both approaches and effectively leveraging low ╧ГantesubscriptЁЭЬОante\sigma_{\text{ante}}italic_╧Г start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ante end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and low ╧ГpostsubscriptЁЭЬОpost\sigma_{\text{post}}italic_╧Г start_POSTSUBSCRIPT post end_POSTSUBSCRIPT to enhance performance.

5.2 Real-World Benchmarks

We now show that cascade routing outperforms baselines on more realistic benchmarks with quality estimates that can be used in real-world scenarios. We differentiate in our analysis between benchmarks where accurate quality estimation is available and those where it is not.

Accurate Quality Estimation

We first evaluate cascade routing on two benchmarks that allow for accurate quality estimation. First, in the domain of software engineering, it is often easier to generate tests to reproduce specific issues than to fix them. We therefore use SWE-Bench (Jimenez et┬аal., 2024) as a benchmark where accurate post-hoc quality estimation is available. Specifically, we assume that the quality of a modelтАЩs response can be accurately estimated by testing it on the ground-truth test cases. Second, to simulate a use-case where ex-ante quality estimation is accurate, we use the Math and Coder models from the Qwen-2.5 model family (Yang et┬аal., 2024; Hui et┬аal., 2024) and evaluate them on a combination of Minerva Math (Lewkowycz et┬аal., 2022) and LiveCodeBench (Jain et┬аal., 2024). To obtain accurate quality estimates, we incorporate a sampleтАЩs origin benchmark as a feature in the quality estimation model. For all details about the benchmarks, models, and estimators, we refer to Section┬аD.1.

Results

Table┬а2 (left) shows the results for both benchmarks. In SWE-Bench, our methods outperform baseline strategies by up to 14%percent1414\%14 %. As expected, the routing strategy does not outperform the trivial baseline on this benchmark, as ex-ante quality estimates are insufficient. Interestingly, despite perfect post-hoc quality estimation for SWE-Bench, the baseline cascade strategy also performs poorly. This is due to the binary feedback of the quality estimator, which leads the threshold ╧ДЁЭЬП\tauitalic_╧Д of the baseline cascade to either admit all models (╧Д=0ЁЭЬП0\tau=0italic_╧Д = 0) or only correct ones (╧Д>0ЁЭЬП0\tau>0italic_╧Д > 0).

For Minerva Math and LiveCodeBench, the opposite trend holds true. With accurate ex-ante quality estimation, the routing strategy achieves strong performance, surpassing the baseline cascade strategy by 10%percent1010\%10 %. However, the cascade routing strategy still outperforms all methods, highlighting its robustness across diverse benchmarks and quality estimation scenarios. Interestingly, despite poor post-hoc quality estimation, our cascading strategy nearly matches the performance of routing. This suggests that the cascade effectively leverages ex-ante quality estimation to make informed decisions, unlike the baseline cascade.

We highlight that the cost estimator for SWE-Bench is latency-based, computing cost as the time it takes to complete the task. In contrast, the estimator for Minerva Math and LiveCodeBench uses the cost of the generation. Thus, cascade routing can adapt to different cost estimators.

Poor Quality Estimation

We perform experiments on classification and open-form reasoning tasks where there is no known accurate quality estimator. The classification benchmarks include ARC-Challenge (Clark et┬аal., 2018), MMLU-Pro (Wang et┬аal., 2024), and MixEval (Ni et┬аal., 2024). For open-form reasoning tasks, we use MMLU-Pro and GSM8k (Cobbe et┬аal., 2021). In classification, models select a single option representing their answer, with no intermediate reasoning process. In contrast, open-form reasoning allows models to generate their answers after reasoning. In this section, we evaluate two model families consisting of three models, Llama and Gemma, and show similar numbers for the Mistral model family in App.┬аH. We create a quality estimator based on state-of-the-art work Gupta et┬аal. (2024), which uses log probabilities as features. For full details on the benchmarks, models, and cost and quality estimators, we refer to App.┬аD.

Results

Table┬а2 (right) presents the results for the Llama and Gemma model families across both benchmarks. Cascade routing consistently performs on par with or outperforms all baselines, though with much narrower margins reaching up to 1.2%percent1.21.2\%1.2 %. This reduced gain can be attributed to the fact that the quality and cost estimates are very noisy, leading to performance gains over the naive baseline similar to those observed in very high-noise scenarios on RouterBench.

6 Related Work

Routing

Routing is a widely studied problem in machine learning, particularly in the task of directing input queries to specialized models. One of the most common applications of routing is model selection for natural language input queries with a known answer (Chuang et┬аal., 2024; Ding et┬аal., 2024; Hari & Thomson, 2023; Liu et┬аal., 2024; Jang et┬аal., 2023; Nguyen et┬аal., 2024; Sakota et┬аal., 2024; Shnitzer et┬аal., 2023). All these works train a model to predict whether a given model will correctly answer a query. Though the setups in these works are largely similar, they vary in certain specifics, such as the type of input queries or the features used for quality estimation.

Routing is also applied in other areas. For instance, Lu et┬аal. (2024); Ong et┬аal. (2024) use preference data to train a quality estimator, which facilitates routing in scenarios involving real-world user queries where clear ground-truth answers may not exist. Additionally, Chen et┬аal. (2022) employ routing for API selection in multi-label classification tasks, focusing on directing queries to the appropriate API based on task requirements. Similarly, Zhang et┬аal. (2024b) apply routing in software agent environments, directing user issues to the agent most suited to handle them. Finally, Pichlmeier et┬аal. (2024) dynamically routes token generation instead of entire queries, allowing for more fine-grained routing decisions.

Cascading

Cascading techniques are primarily used to reduce inference costs by employing smaller models initially and only cascading to larger models if the smaller ones fail to provide a sufficiently accurate answer. Most often, cascading decisions are based on the smaller modelтАЩs confidence in its own predictions (Chen et┬аal., 2023, 2024; Ram├нrez et┬аal., 2024; Varshney & Baral, 2022). However, alternative techniques also exist. For example, Madaan et┬аal. (2023) propose running models multiple times and measuring the variance in their responses to decide whether to cascade to a larger model.

For classification tasks, early stopping is another cascading strategy (Li et┬аal., 2021; Schuster et┬аal., 2022). In this approach, the cascade halts when a modelтАЩs intermediate layers generate representations that are sufficiently informative to predict the correct class. This reduces computational costs by avoiding the need to process every query through the entire model.

There has also been specific research on quality estimation within cascading frameworks. Gupta et┬аal. (2024) examine various measures of uncertainty in language model answers, evaluating their impact on cascading performance. Meanwhile, Jitkrittum et┬аal. (2023a) explore failure cases in cascading mechanisms that rely on uncertainty, introducing alternative quality measures that enhance cascade efficiency. Furthermore, Xue et┬аal. (2023) apply cascading to majority voting for a single model to obtain a method called dynamic voting: the cascade stops depending on the aggregated answers of all previous model computations. Lastly, (Zhang et┬аal., 2024a) propose the use of multi-objective quality metrics to guide cascading decisions and do not solely focus on accuracy.

All works mentioned here can be seen as an instantiation of the thresholding mechanism outlined in Corollary┬а1 with application-specific quality and cost estimates.

7 Conclusion

In this work, we introduced a novel framework for routing and cascading that enabled us to propose theoretically optimal strategies for both paradigms. Further, we used this analysis to propose a new paradigm for model selection, cascade routing, which combines the benefits of routing and cascading. We showed that cascade routing can significantly outperform its baselines, especially with good quality and cost estimates. We also find that our new cascading strategy significantly outperforms existing approaches to cascading, showing our theoretical analysis also leads to practical gains.

Impact Statement

Our work can significantly impact the field of model selection strategies. By providing a theoretical foundation for routing and cascading, we have shown that these strategies can be improved by using more accurate quality and cost estimates. Cascade routing combines the strengths of both routing and cascading and offers a more flexible and effective model selection strategy. Furthermore, by underscoring the importance of quality estimation, we highlighted a critical area for future research in model selection strategies that could lead to further improvements in this area.

Appendix A Routing

First, we explain how to obtain the hyperparameters ╬╗ЁЭЬЖ\lambdaitalic_╬╗ and ╬│ЁЭЫ╛\gammaitalic_╬│ for the routing strategy. We then provide a more exact formulation of the routing optimization problem and prove Theorem┬а1.

Hyperparameters Due to the second part of Theorem┬а1, we only need to find a set of hyperparameters ╬╗ЁЭЬЖ\lambdaitalic_╬╗ and ╬│ЁЭЫ╛\gammaitalic_╬│ that achieve the cost budget. Indeed, all routing strategies that have an expected cost that is exactly equal to BЁЭР╡Bitalic_B and can be written as a convex combination of smin╬╗тА▓superscriptsubscriptЁЭСаminsuperscriptЁЭЬЖтА▓s_{\textsc{min}}^{\lambda^{\prime}}italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT тА▓ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT and smax╬╗тА▓superscriptsubscriptЁЭСаmaxsuperscriptЁЭЬЖтА▓s_{\textsc{max}}^{\lambda^{\prime}}italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT тА▓ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT for some ╬╗тА▓тИИтДЭ+superscriptЁЭЬЖтА▓superscriptтДЭ\lambda^{\prime}\in\mathbb{R}^{+}italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT тА▓ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT тИИ blackboard_R start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT + end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT achieve the same optimal quality.

To determine these parameters, we estimate the cost of a strategy using a validation dataset DЁЭР╖Ditalic_D that is representative of the query distribution ЁЭТ│ЁЭТ│\mathcal{X}caligraphic_X. We then perform a hyperparameter search to find optimal values of ╬╗ЁЭЬЖ\lambdaitalic_╬╗ and ╬│ЁЭЫ╛\gammaitalic_╬│. By leveraging several properties of routing strategies, one can show that this hyperparameter search can be reduced to a single binary search over ╬╗ЁЭЬЖ\lambdaitalic_╬╗, enabling a quick and efficient hyperparameter optimization process.

Proving the Theorem

To prove Theorem┬а1, we first rewrite the routing optimization problem in Eq.┬а1 as a linear program over functions s:ЁЭТ│тЖТтДЭk:ЁЭСатЖТЁЭТ│superscriptтДЭЁЭСШs:\mathcal{X}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{k}italic_s : caligraphic_X тЖТ blackboard_R start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT instead of functions s:ЁЭТ│тЖТ╬Фk:ЁЭСатЖТЁЭТ│subscript╬ФЁЭСШs:\mathcal{X}\rightarrow\Delta_{k}italic_s : caligraphic_X тЖТ roman_╬Ф start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. This makes the optimization problem more tractable. Specifically, Eq.┬а1 can be rewritten as follows:

maxrsubscriptЁЭСЯ\displaystyle\max_{r}roman_max start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_r end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ЁЭФ╝xтИ╝ЁЭТ│тБв[тИСi=1ksiтБв(x)тБвq^iтБв(x)]subscriptЁЭФ╝similar-toЁЭСеЁЭТ│delimited-[]superscriptsubscriptЁЭСЦ1ЁЭСШsubscriptЁЭСаЁЭСЦЁЭСеsubscript^ЁЭСЮЁЭСЦЁЭСе\displaystyle\mathbb{E}_{x\sim\mathcal{X}}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{k}s_{i}(x)\hat{q}_% {i}(x)\right]blackboard_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_x тИ╝ caligraphic_X end_POSTSUBSCRIPT [ тИС start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i = 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) over^ start_ARG italic_q end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) ] (2)
s.t. ЁЭФ╝xтИ╝ЁЭТ│тБв[тИСi=1ksiтБв(x)тБвc^iтБв(x)]тй╜BsubscriptЁЭФ╝similar-toЁЭСеЁЭТ│delimited-[]superscriptsubscriptЁЭСЦ1ЁЭСШsubscriptЁЭСаЁЭСЦЁЭСеsubscript^ЁЭСРЁЭСЦЁЭСеЁЭР╡\displaystyle\mathbb{E}_{x\sim\mathcal{X}}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{k}s_{i}(x)\hat{c}_% {i}(x)\right]\leqslant Bblackboard_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_x тИ╝ caligraphic_X end_POSTSUBSCRIPT [ тИС start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i = 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) over^ start_ARG italic_c end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) ] тй╜ italic_B
тИАiтИИ{1,тАж,k}:тИАxтИИЁЭТ│:siтБв(x)тЙе0тИзтИСj=1ksjтБв(x)=1:for-allЁЭСЦ1тАжЁЭСШfor-allЁЭСеЁЭТ│:subscriptЁЭСаЁЭСЦЁЭСе0superscriptsubscriptЁЭСЧ1ЁЭСШsubscriptЁЭСаЁЭСЧЁЭСе1\displaystyle\forall i\in\{1,...,k\}:\forall x\in\mathcal{X}:s_{i}(x)\geq 0% \land\sum_{j=1}^{k}s_{j}(x)=1тИА italic_i тИИ { 1 , тАж , italic_k } : тИА italic_x тИИ caligraphic_X : italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) тЙе 0 тИз тИС start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j = 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) = 1

We then rewrite Theorem┬а1 to allow for a more exact formulation of the optimal routing strategy:

Theorem 4.

(Optimal Routing Strategy) Suppose there exists an admissible solution to the set of constraints in Eq.┬а2. For any ╬╗тИИтДЭ+ЁЭЬЖsuperscriptтДЭ\lambda\in\mathbb{R}^{+}italic_╬╗ тИИ blackboard_R start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT + end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT, let S╬╗subscriptЁЭСЖЁЭЬЖS_{\lambda}italic_S start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_╬╗ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT be the set of routing strategies sЁЭСаsitalic_s that satisfy the following constraints:

тИАxтИИЁЭТ│,тИАiтИИ{1,тАж,k}:qi^тБв(x)тИТ╬╗тБвci^тБв(x)<maxjтБбqj^тБв(x)тИТ╬╗тБвcj^тБв(x)тЗТsiтБв(x)=0:formulae-sequencefor-allЁЭСеЁЭТ│for-allЁЭСЦ1тАжЁЭСШ^subscriptЁЭСЮЁЭСЦЁЭСеЁЭЬЖ^subscriptЁЭСРЁЭСЦЁЭСеsubscriptЁЭСЧ^subscriptЁЭСЮЁЭСЧЁЭСеЁЭЬЖ^subscriptЁЭСРЁЭСЧЁЭСетЗТsubscriptЁЭСаЁЭСЦЁЭСе0\forall x\in\mathcal{X},\forall i\in\{1,...,k\}:\hat{q_{i}}(x)-\lambda\hat{c_{% i}}(x)<\max_{j}\hat{q_{j}}(x)-\lambda\hat{c_{j}}(x)\Rightarrow s_{i}(x)=0тИА italic_x тИИ caligraphic_X , тИА italic_i тИИ { 1 , тАж , italic_k } : over^ start_ARG italic_q start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG ( italic_x ) - italic_╬╗ over^ start_ARG italic_c start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG ( italic_x ) < roman_max start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT over^ start_ARG italic_q start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG ( italic_x ) - italic_╬╗ over^ start_ARG italic_c start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG ( italic_x ) тЗТ italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) = 0 (3)

If there exists a strategy in S0subscriptЁЭСЖ0S_{0}italic_S start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT that has a cost less than or equal to BЁЭР╡Bitalic_B, then this strategy achieves the optimal quality. Otherwise, there exists a ╬╗тИЧтИИтДЭ+superscriptЁЭЬЖsuperscriptтДЭ\lambda^{*}\in\mathbb{R}^{+}italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT тИЧ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT тИИ blackboard_R start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT + end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT such that S╬╗subscriptЁЭСЖЁЭЬЖS_{\lambda}italic_S start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_╬╗ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT contains a routing strategy that has exactly cost BЁЭР╡Bitalic_B and all routing strategies in тЛГ╬╗тИИтДЭ+S╬╗subscriptЁЭЬЖsuperscriptтДЭsubscriptЁЭСЖЁЭЬЖ\bigcup_{\lambda\in\mathbb{R}^{+}}S_{\lambda}тЛГ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_╬╗ тИИ blackboard_R start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT + end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_S start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_╬╗ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT that have cost BЁЭР╡Bitalic_B achieve the same optimal quality.

There is one extra condition mentioned here that we omitted in the main text. The requirement of having at least an admissible solution to the constraints in Eq.┬а2 is necessary to ensure that the set of possible solutions to Eq.┬а2 is not empty. For instance, the cost budget BЁЭР╡Bitalic_B can be too low such that even running the cheapest model for each query is too expensive.

The formulation of soptsubscriptЁЭСаopts_{\textsc{opt}}italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT opt end_POSTSUBSCRIPT as a convex combination of smin╬╗superscriptsubscriptЁЭСаminЁЭЬЖs_{\textsc{min}}^{\lambda}italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_╬╗ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT and smax╬╗superscriptsubscriptЁЭСаmaxЁЭЬЖs_{\textsc{max}}^{\lambda}italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_╬╗ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT is a direct consequence of Theorem┬а4. Indeed, let ╬╗тИЧsuperscriptЁЭЬЖ\lambda^{*}italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT тИЧ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT be as defined in Theorem┬а4. Then smin╬╗тИЧsuperscriptsubscriptЁЭСаminsuperscriptЁЭЬЖs_{\textsc{min}}^{\lambda^{*}}italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT тИЧ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT, resp. smax╬╗тИЧsuperscriptsubscriptЁЭСаmaxsuperscriptЁЭЬЖs_{\textsc{max}}^{\lambda^{*}}italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT тИЧ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT, must have the lowest, resp. highest, cost among all routing strategies in S╬╗тИЧsubscriptЁЭСЖsuperscriptЁЭЬЖS_{\lambda^{*}}italic_S start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT тИЧ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. Since there is a routing strategy in S╬╗тИЧsubscriptЁЭСЖsuperscriptЁЭЬЖS_{\lambda^{*}}italic_S start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT тИЧ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_POSTSUBSCRIPT that has cost BЁЭР╡Bitalic_B, there must exist a convex combination of smin╬╗тИЧsuperscriptsubscriptЁЭСаminsuperscriptЁЭЬЖs_{\textsc{min}}^{\lambda^{*}}italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT тИЧ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT and smax╬╗тИЧsuperscriptsubscriptЁЭСаmaxsuperscriptЁЭЬЖs_{\textsc{max}}^{\lambda^{*}}italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT тИЧ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT that also has cost BЁЭР╡Bitalic_B and thus achieves the optimal quality.

We first prove several lemmas before proving the theorem.

Lemma 2.

S╬╗subscriptЁЭСЖЁЭЬЖS_{\lambda}italic_S start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_╬╗ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is non-empty and convex for all ╬╗тИИтДЭ+ЁЭЬЖsuperscriptтДЭ\lambda\in\mathbb{R}^{+}italic_╬╗ тИИ blackboard_R start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT + end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT.

Proof.

Non-emptiness follows from the fact that the routing strategy that assigns all probability mass for a sample xЁЭСеxitalic_x to a model iЁЭСЦiitalic_i for which qi^тБв(x)тИТ╬╗тБвci^тБв(x)^subscriptЁЭСЮЁЭСЦЁЭСеЁЭЬЖ^subscriptЁЭСРЁЭСЦЁЭСе\hat{q_{i}}(x)-\lambda\hat{c_{i}}(x)over^ start_ARG italic_q start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG ( italic_x ) - italic_╬╗ over^ start_ARG italic_c start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG ( italic_x ) is maximal, is in S╬╗subscriptЁЭСЖЁЭЬЖS_{\lambda}italic_S start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_╬╗ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. For convexity, let s(1),s(2)тИИS╬╗superscriptЁЭСа1superscriptЁЭСа2subscriptЁЭСЖЁЭЬЖs^{(1)},s^{(2)}\in S_{\lambda}italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 1 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT тИИ italic_S start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_╬╗ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT be arbitrary. Let s╬│superscriptЁЭСаЁЭЫ╛s^{\gamma}italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_╬│ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT be the convex combination of s(1)superscriptЁЭСа1s^{(1)}italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 1 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT and s(2)superscriptЁЭСа2s^{(2)}italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT with weight ╬│тИИ[0,1]ЁЭЫ╛01\gamma\in[0,1]italic_╬│ тИИ [ 0 , 1 ]. Let xтИИЁЭТ│ЁЭСеЁЭТ│x\in\mathcal{X}italic_x тИИ caligraphic_X be arbitrary. Then, si╬│тБв(x)>0superscriptsubscriptЁЭСаЁЭСЦЁЭЫ╛ЁЭСе0s_{i}^{\gamma}(x)>0italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_╬│ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x ) > 0 if and only if si(1)тБв(x)>0superscriptsubscriptЁЭСаЁЭСЦ1ЁЭСе0s_{i}^{(1)}(x)>0italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 1 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x ) > 0 or si(2)тБв(x)>0superscriptsubscriptЁЭСаЁЭСЦ2ЁЭСе0s_{i}^{(2)}(x)>0italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x ) > 0. Since s(1),s(2)тИИS╬╗superscriptЁЭСа1superscriptЁЭСа2subscriptЁЭСЖЁЭЬЖs^{(1)},s^{(2)}\in S_{\lambda}italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 1 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT тИИ italic_S start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_╬╗ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, we have qi^тБв(x)тИТ╬╗тБвci^тБв(x)тй╛maxjтБбqj^тБв(x)тИТ╬╗тБвcj^тБв(x)^subscriptЁЭСЮЁЭСЦЁЭСеЁЭЬЖ^subscriptЁЭСРЁЭСЦЁЭСеsubscriptЁЭСЧ^subscriptЁЭСЮЁЭСЧЁЭСеЁЭЬЖ^subscriptЁЭСРЁЭСЧЁЭСе\hat{q_{i}}(x)-\lambda\hat{c_{i}}(x)\geqslant\max_{j}\hat{q_{j}}(x)-\lambda% \hat{c_{j}}(x)over^ start_ARG italic_q start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG ( italic_x ) - italic_╬╗ over^ start_ARG italic_c start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG ( italic_x ) тй╛ roman_max start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT over^ start_ARG italic_q start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG ( italic_x ) - italic_╬╗ over^ start_ARG italic_c start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG ( italic_x ) for all iЁЭСЦiitalic_i such that si(1)тБв(x)>0superscriptsubscriptЁЭСаЁЭСЦ1ЁЭСе0s_{i}^{(1)}(x)>0italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 1 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x ) > 0 or si(2)тБв(x)>0superscriptsubscriptЁЭСаЁЭСЦ2ЁЭСе0s_{i}^{(2)}(x)>0italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x ) > 0. This implies that qi^тБв(x)тИТ╬╗тБвci^тБв(x)тй╛maxjтБбqj^тБв(x)тИТ╬╗тБвcj^тБв(x)^subscriptЁЭСЮЁЭСЦЁЭСеЁЭЬЖ^subscriptЁЭСРЁЭСЦЁЭСеsubscriptЁЭСЧ^subscriptЁЭСЮЁЭСЧЁЭСеЁЭЬЖ^subscriptЁЭСРЁЭСЧЁЭСе\hat{q_{i}}(x)-\lambda\hat{c_{i}}(x)\geqslant\max_{j}\hat{q_{j}}(x)-\lambda% \hat{c_{j}}(x)over^ start_ARG italic_q start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG ( italic_x ) - italic_╬╗ over^ start_ARG italic_c start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG ( italic_x ) тй╛ roman_max start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT over^ start_ARG italic_q start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG ( italic_x ) - italic_╬╗ over^ start_ARG italic_c start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG ( italic_x ) for all iЁЭСЦiitalic_i such that si╬│тБв(x)>0superscriptsubscriptЁЭСаЁЭСЦЁЭЫ╛ЁЭСе0s_{i}^{\gamma}(x)>0italic_s start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_╬│ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x ) > 0. Thus, s╬│тИИS╬╗superscriptЁЭСаЁЭЫ╛subscriptЁЭСЖЁЭЬЖs^{\gamma}\in S_{\lambda}italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_╬│ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT тИИ italic_S start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_╬╗ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. тИО

Lemma 3.

Let ╬╗1<╬╗2subscriptЁЭЬЖ1subscriptЁЭЬЖ2\lambda_{1}<\lambda_{2}italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT < italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and s(1)superscriptЁЭСа1s^{(1)}italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 1 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT, resp. s(2)superscriptЁЭСа2s^{(2)}italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT be arbitrary routing strategies in S╬╗1subscriptЁЭСЖsubscriptЁЭЬЖ1S_{\lambda_{1}}italic_S start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, resp. S╬╗2subscriptЁЭСЖsubscriptЁЭЬЖ2S_{\lambda_{2}}italic_S start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_╬╗ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. Then, the cost of s(1)superscriptЁЭСа1s^{(1)}italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 1 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT is greater or equal to the cost of s(2)superscriptЁЭСа2s^{(2)}italic_s start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT, i.e.,
